ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed fish food product class; no specific Thai lime steamed fish entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers steamed fish generically; no Thai-specific lime-steamed fish code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai steamed fish with lime as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: whole fish (typically sea bass or tilapia)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Thai steamed fish with lime is approximately 350 g, comprising a whole or half fish (sea bass or tilapia) with lime-chilli-fish sauce dressing, served as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, steamed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15264 / analogous steamed lean white fish) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai steamed fish preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for steamed lean white fish (sea bass / tilapia) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Thai steamed fish dishes. The lime-chilli-fish sauce dressing contributes minimal macronutrients but adds sodium. Values represent the composite dish per 100 g edible portion. Sodium is elevated relative to plain steamed fish due to fish sauce seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for steamed fish with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai steamed fish with lime. Steamed lean fish dishes contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (approximately 2.4 g per 100 g), yielding a very low glycemic response. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with other low-carbohydrate fish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (5 × 8.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.4, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, which represents 64–98% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving, Thai steamed fish with lime has negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable high-protein, low-GI main course for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein, low saturated fat, beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed white fish provides lean protein with low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids; however, the high sodium content from fish sauce warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are sodium-restricted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Steamed Fish with Lime (ปลานึ่งมะนาว, Pla Nueng Manao) is a classic Thai seafood dish in which a whole or filleted white fish — most commonly sea bass (pla krapong) or tilapia — is steamed over aromatics such as lemongrass and galangal, then dressed with a vibrant sauce of fresh lime juice, fish sauce, minced garlic, fresh bird's eye chilli, and coriander. The dish is a staple of Thai home cooking and restaurant menus alike, prized for its clean, bright flavour profile: intensely sour from lime, savoury-umami from fish sauce, mildly spicy from chilli, and fragrant from fresh herbs. The texture of the fish is delicate, moist, and flaky due to the gentle steaming method, which preserves natural juices and avoids the addition of cooking fats. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 17.8 g per 100 g), very low in fat (2.1 g per 100 g), and extremely low in carbohydrate (2.4 g per 100 g), making it one of the most nutritionally favourable Thai restaurant dishes available. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible (GL < 1). The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce; a full restaurant serving (approximately 350 g) may contain 1400–1500 mg sodium. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and weight, though sodium-restricted individuals should moderate fish sauce quantity. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants and is available on food delivery platforms at premium price points.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Thai steamed fish with lime exists in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy steamed lean white fish data and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. The lime-chilli-fish sauce dressing contribution to sodium is estimated based on standard Thai recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the composite dish (fish plus lime-chilli-fish sauce dressing) per 100 g edible portion, adapted from USDA SR Legacy steamed lean white fish and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. 2. Sodium is elevated relative to plain steamed fish due to fish sauce seasoning; the value of 420 mg per 100 g is an estimate based on standard Thai recipe proportions and may vary by restaurant. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for steamed lean white fish per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero for this preparation method. 4. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with low-carbohydrate steamed fish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for steamed sea bass / tilapia and are not individually confirmed from a single primary source for this Thai preparation. 6. The food_name has been normalised to 'Steamed Fish with Lime' as the canonical English name; 'Healthy' is a promotional qualifier not suitable for a canonical food name. 7. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are null as this is a Thai dish; the mandatory multilingual requirement for Thai cuisine requires only the Thai-script name, which is present. 8. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 is used as closest parent for steamed fish food product; no Thai-specific lime-steamed fish class exists in FoodOn as of the processing date. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, steamed) is used as closest parent; verified against EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue.
